ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Milk-based desserts) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for osmanthus almond pudding.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for osmanthus almond pudding or sufficiently close almond pudding variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g (one bowl or cup), as typically served in Singaporean Chinese dessert shops.

nutrient_source.title: Almond Jelly / Almond Tofu — estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient components (apricot kernel paste, whole milk, sugar, agar) and HPB Singapore nutrient reference values for Chinese milk-based desserts
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for osmanthus almond pudding exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by ingredient-level composition: southern almonds (apricot kernels, USDA FDC #170567), whole milk (USDA FDC #746782), sugar (USDA FDC #169655), and agar (USDA FDC #168869), combined in proportions consistent with a standard almond pudding recipe (approximately 8% almond paste, 60% milk, 10% sugar, 1% agar, remainder water). Energy and macros cross-checked against HPB Singapore reference values for similar Chinese milk-based desserts. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk-based desserts and almond-containing puddings — analogous estimate based on dairy-based pudding GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and published literature on almond-containing desserts
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for osmanthus almond pudding. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous milk-based pudding and almond jelly entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Dairy-based puddings typically range GI 35–50; the presence of almonds (low GI ~15) and agar (low GI) moderates the overall GI downward. GL per serving calculated as (40 × 16.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 13.2 g for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~38 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Osmanthus almond pudding is low in sodium (~76 mg per 200 g serving) and poses minimal hypertension risk, though individuals monitoring added sugar intake should note the moderate sugar content of approximately 28 g per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content and estimated low-to-moderate glycemic index (~40)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 40 and approximately 28 g of sugar per 200 g serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume osmanthus almond pudding in moderation and account for the glycemic load of ~13 g per serving within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and presence of heart-beneficial almonds
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Osmanthus almond pudding contains modest saturated fat (~1.2 g per 100 g) and benefits from the inclusion of southern almonds, which provide monounsaturated fats and vitamin E; occasional consumption is unlikely to adversely affect cardiovascular risk in otherwise healthy individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Osmanthus Almond Pudding (桂花杏仁豆腐, Guìhuā Xìngrén Dòufu) is a classic Chinese dessert widely enjoyed in Singapore and southern China. It is made by slowly simmering southern almonds — botanically the sweet kernels of apricot seeds (Prunus armeniaca), distinct from Western almonds — into a smooth, fragrant paste, which is then combined with milk, sugar, and a setting agent such as agar or gelatin to form a silky, tender pudding. The dessert is garnished with dried or fresh osmanthus blossoms (桂花, Guìhuā), which impart a delicate floral fragrance characteristic of Cantonese and Shanghainese sweet soups. The texture is smooth, soft, and lightly yielding — often described as melting in the mouth — with a mild, nutty almond flavour and a subtle floral finish. Nutritionally, a 200 g serving provides approximately 196 kcal, 5.6 g protein, 5.0 g fat, and 33 g carbohydrate, with a moderate sugar content of ~28 g. The estimated glycemic index is 40 (low), making it a relatively moderate dessert choice compared to high-sugar confections. It contains milk and tree nuts (almonds) as allergens. Clinically, it is low in sodium and saturated fat, but the sugar content warrants moderation for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. It is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and gluten-free. In Singapore, it is commonly served at Chinese dessert shops (糖水铺, tángshuǐ pù) as a chilled after-meal sweet.
common_user_queries: Is osmanthus almond pudding suitable for diabetics?
桂花杏仁豆腐 营养

confidence_reason: No exact entry for osmanthus almond pudding was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from ingredient-level composition using USDA FDC data for southern almonds, whole milk, sugar, and agar, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore reference values for similar Chinese milk-based desserts. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the estimation methodology rather than a direct source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ingredient composition (USDA FDC) and cross-checked against HPB Singapore reference values for Chinese milk-based desserts; no exact dish-level entry was found in any approved primary source. 2. 'Southern almonds' (南杏仁) used in this dish are the sweet kernels of Prunus armeniaca (apricot), not Western almonds (Prunus dulcis); they have a milder flavour and are used in traditional Chinese medicine and desserts. 3. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on dairy-based pudding and almond jelly entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in milk-based desserts at this composition level; naturally zero by product formulation for the non-dairy components. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.3) are estimated from almond and milk fat composition via USDA FDC. 6. All -1 sentinel values have been replaced with estimated numeric values where defensible ingredient-level data exists; no -1 values remain in this record. 7. Serving size of 200 g is based on typical Chinese dessert shop portion sizes in Singapore. 8. Halal status is assumed based on typical ingredients; individual restaurant preparation may vary.
